query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 4, at what point is an estate agent considered to have entered into a business relationship with a purchaser?
reference_14b: An estate agent is considered to have entered into a business relationship with a purchaser under MLR 2017 Regulation 4 at the point when the purchaser's offer is accepted by the seller.
reference_72b: An estate agent is considered to have entered into a business relationship with a purchaser at the point when the purchaser's offer is accepted by the seller.

query: What ownership or voting rights threshold must an individual exceed to be classified as a beneficial owner of a non-listed body corporate under MLR 2017 regulation 5?
reference_14b: An individual must ultimately own or control more than 25% of the shares or voting rights in the body corporate, either directly or indirectly, to be classified as a beneficial owner under MLR 2017 regulation 5.
reference_72b: An individual must own or control more than 25% of the shares or voting rights in the body corporate to be classified as a beneficial owner of a non-listed body corporate under MLR 2017 regulation 5.

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 6, which categories of persons are treated as beneficial owners of a trust?
reference_14b: Under MLR 2017 Regulation 6, the following categories of persons are treated as beneficial owners of a trust: the settlor, the trustees, the beneficiaries, the class of persons in whose main interest the trust is set up or operates if the beneficiaries have not been determined, and any individual who has control over the trust.
reference_72b: Under MLR 2017 regulation 6, the categories of persons treated as beneficial owners of a trust include the settlor, the trustees, the beneficiaries, the class of persons in whose main interest the trust is set up or operates if the beneficiaries have not been determined, and any individual who has control over the trust.

query: At what cash payment level does a trader become a 'high value dealer' for the purposes of MLR 2017, and does it matter whether the payment is made in a single operation or multiple linked operations?
reference_14b: A trader becomes a 'high value dealer' under MLR 2017 if they make or receive a payment or payments in cash totaling at least 10,000 euros in a single operation or in several linked operations.
reference_72b: A trader becomes a 'high value dealer' for the purposes of MLR 2017 when they make or receive a payment or payments in cash of at least 10,000 euros in total, regardless of whether the transaction is executed in a single operation or in several operations which appear to be linked.

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 27, in what circumstances is a relevant person required to apply customer due diligence measures?
reference_14b: A relevant person must apply customer due diligence measures if they establish a business relationship, carry out an occasional transaction amounting to a transfer of funds exceeding 1,000 euros, suspect money laundering or terrorist financing, or doubt the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained identification or verification documents. Additionally, a relevant person who is not a high value dealer or casino must apply customer due diligence measures for occasional transactions of 15,000 euros or more, while a high value dealer must apply these measures for cash transactions of 10,000 euros or more. Casinos must apply customer due diligence measures for transactions of 2,000 euros or more related to wagering stakes or collecting winnings. Customer due diligence measures must also be applied at other appropriate times to existing customers on a risk-based approach or when changes in customer circumstances are identified.
reference_72b: A relevant person must apply customer due diligence measures when establishing a business relationship, carrying out an occasional transaction exceeding 1,000 euros that involves a transfer of funds, suspecting money laundering or terrorist financing, or doubting the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained identification or verification information. Additionally, for transactions of 15,000 euros or more (for non-high value dealers and non-casinos), 10,000 euros or more in cash (for high value dealers), and 2,000 euros or more (for casinos involving specific gambling transactions). Customer due diligence measures must also be applied at other appropriate times to existing customers based on a risk-based approach, and when the relevant person becomes aware of changes in the customer's circumstances.

query: What three obligations does MLR 2017 regulation 28(2) impose on a relevant person when it applies customer due diligence measures to a new customer?
reference_14b: Regulation 28(2) imposes three obligations on a relevant person when applying customer due diligence measures: identify the customer, verify the customer's identity, and assess and obtain information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship or occasional transaction.
reference_72b: When applying customer due diligence measures to a new customer, a relevant person must: (1) identify the customer unless the customer's identity is already known and verified, (2) verify the customer’s identity unless it has already been verified, and (3) assess and, where appropriate, obtain information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship or occasional transaction.

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 30, when must verification of a customer's identity ordinarily be completed relative to the establishment of a business relationship?
reference_14b: Verification of a customer's identity must be completed before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of the transaction, unless specific exceptions apply.
reference_72b: Verification of a customer's identity must ordinarily be completed before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of the transaction.

query: What conditions must a relevant person assess before applying simplified customer due diligence measures under MLR 2017 regulation 37?
reference_14b: Before applying simplified customer due diligence measures under MLR 2017 regulation 37, a relevant person must determine that the business relationship or transaction presents a low degree of risk of money laundering and terrorist financing, taking into account the risk assessment conducted under regulation 18(1), relevant information made available under regulations 17(9) and 47, and the risk factors specified in paragraph (3).
reference_72b: Before applying simplified customer due diligence measures, a relevant person must assess that the business relationship or transaction presents a low degree of risk of money laundering and terrorist financing, based on their risk assessment under regulation 18(1), relevant information under regulations 17(9) and 47, and the risk factors specified in paragraph (3).

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 38, what maximum electronically stored amount allows a payment product to be exempt from customer due diligence requirements, and does the exemption limit differ for UK-only use products?
reference_14b: A relevant person is not required to apply customer due diligence measures if the maximum amount stored electronically is 250 euros, or 500 euros if the amount stored can only be used in the United Kingdom.
reference_72b: The maximum amount that can be stored electronically to be exempt from customer due diligence requirements is 250 euros. However, if the amount stored can only be used in the United Kingdom, the limit is 500 euros.

query: What systemic obligation does MLR 2017 regulation 35 impose on relevant persons regarding risk-management systems for politically exposed persons?
reference_14b: Relevant persons must have in place appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to determine if a customer or the beneficial owner of a customer is a politically exposed person (PEP), a family member, or a known close associate of a PEP, and to manage the enhanced risks arising from their business relationships or transactions with such customers.
reference_72b: Relevant persons must have appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to determine if a customer or beneficial owner is a politically exposed person (PEP) or a family member or known close associate of a PEP, and to manage the enhanced risks from business relationships or transactions with such individuals.

query: What records concerning beneficial owners must the trustees of a relevant trust maintain under MLR 2017 regulation 44?
reference_14b: The trustees of a relevant trust must maintain accurate and up-to-date records in writing of all the beneficial owners of the trust, and of any potential beneficiaries referred to in paragraph (5)(b), containing the information required by regulation 45(2)(b) to (d) and (5)(f) and (g).
reference_72b: The trustees of a relevant trust must maintain accurate and up-to-date written records of all the beneficial owners of the trust, including the information required by regulation 45(2)(b) to (d) and (5)(f) and (g).

query: Under MLR 2017 regulation 29, when must a credit institution or financial institution identify the beneficiary of a long-term insurance policy?
reference_14b: A credit institution or financial institution must identify the beneficiaries of a long-term insurance policy as soon as they are identified or designated, and verify their identities before any payment is made under the insurance policy.
reference_72b: A credit institution or financial institution must identify the beneficiary of a long-term insurance policy as soon as the beneficiaries are identified or designated, and they must obtain sufficient information to establish the identity of the beneficiary before any payment is made under the insurance policy.

query: What information must a credit institution gather about a third-country respondent institution under MLR 2017 regulation 34 before establishing a correspondent relationship?
reference_14b: A credit institution must gather sufficient information about the third-country respondent institution to understand fully the nature of its business, determine its reputation and the quality of supervision it is subject to from publicly-available credible sources, assess its controls to counter money laundering and terrorist financing, and ensure that the respondent verifies the identity of and conducts ongoing customer due diligence on customers with direct access to accounts with the correspondent.
reference_72b: A credit institution must gather sufficient information about the third-country respondent institution to understand fully the nature of its business, determine the respondent's reputation and the quality of the supervision it is subject to from publicly-available information from credible sources, assess the respondent’s controls to counter money laundering and terrorist financing, and ensure that the respondent can verify the identity of and conduct ongoing customer due diligence measures for customers with direct access to accounts with the correspondent.

query: What five acts constitute the principal money laundering concealment offence under section 327 of POCA 2002?
reference_14b: The five acts that constitute the principal money laundering concealment offence under section 327 of POCA 2002 are: concealing criminal property, disguising criminal property, converting criminal property, transferring criminal property, and removing criminal property from England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland.
reference_72b: The five acts that constitute the principal money laundering concealment offence under section 327 of POCA 2002 are: concealing criminal property, disguising criminal property, converting criminal property, transferring criminal property, and removing criminal property from England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland.

query: How does section 328 of POCA 2002 define the arrangement offence, and what is the required mental element regarding the nature of the property?
reference_14b: Section 328 of POCA 2002 defines the arrangement offence as entering into or becoming concerned in an arrangement which one knows or suspects facilitates the acquisition, retention, use or control of criminal property by or on behalf of another person. The required mental element regarding the nature of the property is that the person must know or suspect that the property involved is criminal property.
reference_72b: Section 328 of POCA 2002 defines the arrangement offence as entering into or becoming concerned in an arrangement which the person knows or suspects facilitates the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property by or on behalf of another person. The required mental element is that the person must know or suspect that the property is criminal property.

query: Under section 329 of POCA 2002, what three types of conduct with criminal property constitute the acquisition, use and possession offence?
reference_14b: Under section 329 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, the three types of conduct with criminal property that constitute the acquisition, use and possession offence are acquiring criminal property, using criminal property, and having possession of criminal property.
reference_72b: The three types of conduct that constitute the acquisition, use and possession offence under section 329 of POCA 2002 are: acquiring criminal property, using criminal property, and having possession of criminal property.

query: What three conditions must all be satisfied for a person to commit the failure-to-disclose offence under section 330 of POCA 2002?
reference_14b: For a person to commit the failure-to-disclose offence under section 330 of POCA 2002, all of the following three conditions must be satisfied: the person knows or suspects, or has reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering; the information or other matter on which their knowledge or suspicion is based came to them in the course of a business in the regulated sector; and the person can identify the other person or the whereabouts of the laundered property, or believes, or it is reasonable to expect them to believe, that the information will or may assist in identifying that other person or the whereabouts of the property. The offence itself is committed if, despite these three conditions being met, the person does not make the required disclosure to a nominated officer or a person authorised by the Director General of the National Crime Agency as soon as is practicable.
reference_72b: To commit the failure-to-disclose offence under section 330 of POCA 2002, a person must satisfy the following three conditions: (1) they know or suspect, or have reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering; (2) the information or matter on which their knowledge or suspicion is based came to them in the course of a business in the regulated sector; (3) they can identify the other person or the whereabouts of the laundered property, or they believe, or it is reasonable to expect them to believe, that the information will or may assist in identifying the other person or the whereabouts of the laundered property. The offence is committed when, despite meeting these conditions, the person does not make the required disclosure to a nominated officer or a person authorised by the Director General of the National Crime Agency as soon as is practicable.

query: Under section 331 of POCA 2002, who specifically commits an offence by failing to disclose knowledge or suspicion of money laundering, and what are the conditions of that offence?
reference_14b: A person nominated to receive disclosures under section 330 commits an offence if they know or suspect, or have reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering, the information or matter giving rise to their knowledge or suspicion came from a disclosure under section 330, they know the identity of the other person or the whereabouts of laundered property from the disclosure, and they fail to make the required disclosure to a person authorized by the Director General of the National Crime Agency as soon as is practicable.
reference_72b: A person nominated to receive disclosures under section 330 commits an offence if they know or suspect, or have reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering, and the information leading to this knowledge or suspicion came from a section 330 disclosure. Additionally, they must know or reasonably believe that the information will assist in identifying the person or the whereabouts of the laundered property, and they fail to make the required disclosure to an authorised person as soon as is practicable.

query: What three elements must be present for a person to commit the tipping off offence in the regulated sector under section 333A of POCA 2002?
reference_14b: For a person to commit the tipping off offence in the regulated sector under section 333A of POCA 2002, three elements must be present: a) the person discloses certain matters as defined in subsection (2), b) the disclosure is likely to prejudice an investigation that might follow the disclosure, and c) the information on which the disclosure is based came to the person in the course of a business in the regulated sector.
reference_72b: For a person to commit the tipping off offence in the regulated sector under section 333A of POCA 2002, the following three elements must be present: the person discloses a matter related to a disclosure made under this Part to a constable, an officer of Revenue and Customs, a nominated officer, or a National Crime Agency officer; the disclosure is likely to prejudice any investigation that might be conducted following the initial disclosure; and the information on which the disclosure is based came to the person in the course of a business in the regulated sector.

query: How does section 335 of POCA 2002 define 'appropriate consent', and when is a person treated as having received it in the absence of an explicit response?
reference_14b: Section 335 defines 'appropriate consent' as the consent of a nominated officer, constable, or customs officer to do a prohibited act if an authorized disclosure is made to them. A person is treated as having the appropriate consent if they make an authorized disclosure to a constable or customs officer and either do not receive notice of refusal within the notice period or receive such notice but the moratorium period has expired. The notice period is seven working days, and the moratorium period is 31 days unless extended by court order or due to ongoing legal proceedings.
reference_72b: Appropriate consent is defined as the consent of a nominated officer, constable, or customs officer to do a prohibited act, following an authorised disclosure to them. A person is treated as having received appropriate consent if they make an authorised disclosure to a constable or customs officer and do not receive a refusal notice within the notice period, which is seven working days.

query: Under section 336 of POCA 2002, what conditions must be satisfied before a nominated officer may give appropriate consent to a prohibited act?
reference_14b: A nominated officer may give appropriate consent to a prohibited act if one of three conditions is met: (1) they make a disclosure to an authorized person who then consents to the act; (2) they make a disclosure and do not receive notice of refusal within the notice period; (3) they make a disclosure, receive notice of refusal within the notice period, but the moratorium period has since expired.
reference_72b: A nominated officer may give appropriate consent to a prohibited act if one of the following conditions is satisfied: (1) the officer makes a disclosure to an authorized person and that person gives consent; (2) the officer makes a disclosure to an authorized person and does not receive a refusal within the notice period; (3) the officer makes a disclosure to an authorized person, receives a refusal within the notice period, and the moratorium period has expired.

query: What three conditions must be met for a disclosure to qualify as a protected disclosure under section 337 of POCA 2002?
reference_14b: The three conditions that must be met for a disclosure to qualify as a protected disclosure under section 337 of POCA 2002 are: the information or matter disclosed must come to the discloser in the course of their trade, profession, business, or employment; the information or matter must cause the discloser to know or suspect, or give them reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering; and the disclosure must be made to a constable, customs officer, or nominated officer as soon as is practicable after the information or matter comes to the discloser.
reference_72b: For a disclosure to qualify as a protected disclosure under section 337 of POCA 2002, it must meet the following three conditions: 1) The information or other matter disclosed must have come to the discloser in the course of their trade, profession, business, or employment. 2) The information or other matter must cause the discloser to know or suspect, or give them reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering. 3) The disclosure must be made to a constable, a customs officer, or a nominated officer as soon as is practicable after the information or other matter comes to the discloser.

query: Under section 338 of POCA 2002, what is the first condition for an authorised disclosure, and what is the significance of the timing of the disclosure relative to the prohibited act?
reference_14b: The first condition for an authorised disclosure under section 338 of POCA 2002 is that the disclosure must be made before the alleged offender commits the prohibited act. The significance of the timing of the disclosure is that it must precede the commission of the prohibited act to satisfy this condition.
reference_72b: The first condition for an authorised disclosure under section 338 of POCA 2002 is that the disclosure is made before the alleged offender does the prohibited act. The significance of the timing is that the disclosure must precede the prohibited act to satisfy this condition.

query: How does section 340 of POCA 2002 define 'criminal property', and what knowledge or suspicion must the alleged offender possess with regard to the property?
reference_14b: Section 340 defines 'criminal property' as property that constitutes a person's benefit from criminal conduct or represents such a benefit, provided the alleged offender knows or suspects that the property constitutes or represents such a benefit.
reference_72b: Criminal property is defined as property that constitutes or represents a benefit from criminal conduct, and the alleged offender must know or suspect that the property constitutes or represents such a benefit.

query: Under section 339 of POCA 2002, who has power to prescribe the form and manner of disclosures, and which sections' disclosures are subject to that prescription?
reference_14b: The Secretary of State has the power to prescribe the form and manner in which disclosures under sections 330, 331, 332, or 338 must be made.
reference_72b: The Secretary of State has the power to prescribe the form and manner of disclosures under sections 330, 331, 332, or 338.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 3 defines 'beneficial owner' for a body corporate by cross-referring to another regulation — which regulation contains that definition, and what ownership threshold does it prescribe?
reference_14b: The definition of 'beneficial owner' for a body corporate is provided in regulation 5(1). It prescribes an ownership threshold of more than 25% of the shares or voting rights in the body corporate.
reference_72b: The definition of 'beneficial owner' for a body corporate is provided in regulation 5. It prescribes an ownership threshold of more than 25% of the shares or voting rights in the body corporate.

query: Regulation 3 of MLR 2017 states that 'business relationship' has the meaning given by another regulation — which regulation defines it, and what two conditions must a relationship satisfy?
reference_14b: Regulation 4 defines 'business relationship'. It must arise out of the business of the relevant person and be expected to have an element of duration at the time contact is established.
reference_72b: The term 'business relationship' is defined by regulation 4. A business relationship must arise out of the business of the relevant person and be expected by the relevant person, at the time when contact is established, to have an element of duration.

query: Regulation 3 of MLR 2017 defines 'customer due diligence measures' as the measures required by a specific regulation — what does that regulation require a relevant person to do when identifying a customer?
reference_14b: When identifying a customer, a relevant person must identify the customer unless the identity of that customer is known to, and has been verified by, the relevant person.
reference_72b: When identifying a customer, a relevant person must identify the customer unless the identity of that customer is already known to and verified by the relevant person. They must also verify the customer’s identity unless it has already been verified.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 3 provides that 'politically exposed person' has the meaning given by a different regulation — which regulation contains that definition, and what systemic obligation does it impose on relevant persons?
reference_14b: Regulation 35 of MLR 2017 defines 'politically exposed person' or 'PEP'. It imposes a systemic obligation on relevant persons to have appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to determine if a customer or beneficial owner is a PEP, their family member, or a known close associate of a PEP, and to manage the enhanced risks arising from such relationships.
reference_72b: The term 'politically exposed person' is defined in regulation 35(12). This regulation imposes the obligation on relevant persons to have appropriate risk-management systems and procedures to identify PEPs and their family members or close associates, and to manage the enhanced risks associated with business relationships or transactions with such individuals.

query: Regulation 28 of MLR 2017 states it applies when a relevant person is required by another regulation to apply CDD measures — which regulation creates that trigger obligation, and in what circumstances does it require CDD to be applied?
reference_14b: Regulation 27 of MLR 2017 creates the trigger obligation for applying CDD measures. It requires CDD measures to be applied when a relevant person establishes a business relationship, carries out an occasional transaction exceeding 1,000 euros, suspects money laundering or terrorist financing, or doubts the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained identification or verification documents.
reference_72b: Regulation 28 of MLR 2017 applies when a relevant person is required by Regulation 27 to apply customer due diligence measures. Regulation 27 requires CDD measures to be applied in the following circumstances: establishing a business relationship, carrying out an occasional transaction exceeding 1,000 euros, suspecting money laundering or terrorist financing, or doubting the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained identification or verification documents or information.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 29 specifies it applies 'in addition to' the measures in another regulation for credit and financial institutions — which base regulation, and what additional obligations does regulation 29 impose regarding insurance policy beneficiaries?
reference_14b: Regulation 29 applies in addition to regulation 28 for credit institutions and financial institutions. It imposes additional obligations regarding insurance policy beneficiaries, requiring the identification and verification of beneficiaries as soon as they are identified or designated, and ensuring that the identity of beneficiaries can be established before any payment is made under the insurance policy. Additionally, if the rights under the insurance policy are assigned to a new beneficiary, the relevant person must identify the new beneficiary as soon as possible and before any payment is made under the policy.
reference_72b: MLR 2017 regulation 29 applies in addition to regulation 28 for credit institutions and financial institutions. It imposes additional obligations regarding insurance policy beneficiaries, requiring the relevant person to identify and verify the beneficiaries' identities before any payment is made under the insurance policy, and to identify new beneficiaries if the policy rights are assigned.

query: What standard CDD measures required by regulation 28 must a relevant person continue to apply alongside the enhanced measures mandated by regulation 33 of MLR 2017?
reference_14b: A relevant person must continue to apply the standard customer due diligence (CDD) measures required by regulation 28 alongside the enhanced measures mandated by regulation 33. These standard CDD measures include identifying the customer, verifying the customer's identity, and assessing the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship or occasional transaction.
reference_72b: A relevant person must continue to apply the standard CDD measures required by regulation 28, which include identifying and verifying the customer's identity, assessing the purpose and nature of the business relationship, and conducting ongoing monitoring of the business relationship, alongside the enhanced measures mandated by regulation 33.

query: What must a credit institution apply under MLR 2017 regulation 34 in addition to the measures required by regulation 33 when establishing a correspondent relationship with a third-country institution?
reference_14b: A credit institution must gather sufficient information about the respondent to understand the nature of its business, determine the reputation of the respondent and the quality of the supervision to which the respondent is subject, assess the respondent's controls to counter money laundering and terrorist financing, obtain approval from senior management before establishing a new correspondent relationship, document the responsibilities of the respondent and correspondent in the correspondent relationship, and ensure that the respondent verifies the identity of and conducts ongoing customer due diligence measures in relation to customers with direct access to accounts with the correspondent.
reference_72b: A credit institution must gather sufficient information about the third-country institution to understand its business, determine its reputation and the quality of supervision, assess its controls against money laundering and terrorist financing, obtain senior management approval, document the responsibilities of both parties, and ensure the third-country institution verifies and conducts ongoing due diligence on customers with direct access to accounts.

query: How does MLR 2017 regulation 35 on politically exposed persons interact with the enhanced CDD framework in regulation 33, and what risk-management steps must a relevant person take on identifying a PEP?
reference_14b: Under MLR 2017 Regulation 35, a relevant person must identify whether a customer or beneficial owner is a politically exposed person (PEP) or a family member or known close associate of a PEP. Upon identification, they must apply enhanced customer due diligence measures as outlined in Regulation 33, which includes additional verification of the source of wealth and funds, senior management approval, and enhanced ongoing monitoring.
reference_72b: Regulation 35 of the MLR 2017 requires a relevant person to implement risk-management systems and procedures to identify PEPs and their family members or close associates, and to manage the enhanced risks associated with them. Upon identifying a PEP, the relevant person must assess the level of risk and apply enhanced customer due diligence measures as specified in regulation 33. These measures include increased monitoring, verification of information, and understanding the background and financial situation of the customer.

query: Regulation 30 of MLR 2017 sets the timing rule for completing verification — to which specific regulations does it apply, and what is the default timing requirement it establishes?
reference_14b: Regulation 30 applies to regulations 27, 28, and 29. The default timing requirement established is that a relevant person must verify the identity of the customer, any person acting on behalf of the customer, and any beneficial owner of the customer before establishing a business relationship or carrying out a transaction.
reference_72b: Regulation 30 of MLR 2017 applies to the verification requirements under regulations 27, 28, and 29. The default timing requirement is that a relevant person must verify the identity of the customer, any person purporting to act on behalf of the customer, and any beneficial owner of the customer before the establishment of a business relationship or the carrying out of the transaction.

query: A person accused under section 327 of POCA 2002 may rely on an authorised disclosure as a defence — which section of POCA governs such disclosures, and what must the disclosure consist of?
reference_14b: A person accused under section 327 of POCA 2002 may rely on an authorised disclosure as a defence if they disclose to a constable, a customs officer, or a nominated officer that the property is criminal property, provided that the disclosure meets one of the conditions set out in section 338. These conditions include making the disclosure before committing the prohibited act, during the act if the person did not initially know or suspect the property was criminal, or after the act if there was a reasonable excuse for not disclosing earlier.
reference_72b: Section 338 of POCA governs authorised disclosures. The disclosure must be made to a constable, a customs officer, or a nominated officer by the alleged offender, and it must state that the property is criminal property. The disclosure can be made before, during, or after the prohibited act, provided certain conditions are met.

query: What authorised disclosure under POCA 2002 can prevent the section 328 arrangement offence from being committed, and which section sets out the requirements for that disclosure?
reference_14b: An authorised disclosure under section 338 of POCA 2002 can prevent the section 328 arrangement offence from being committed, provided the person has the appropriate consent where required (section 328(2)(a)). Section 338 sets out the requirements: the disclosure must be made to a constable, a customs officer, or a nominated officer, and must satisfy one of three timing conditions — made before the prohibited act, made during the act if the person did not initially know or suspect the property was criminal and discloses on their own initiative as soon as practicable, or made after the act with a reasonable excuse for the delay and as soon as practicable.
reference_72b: An authorised disclosure under section 338 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 can prevent the section 328 arrangement offence from being committed. This disclosure must be made to a constable, customs officer, or nominated officer, and must satisfy one of three conditions: it is made before the prohibited act, during the act if the person began without knowing the property was criminal, or after the act with a reasonable excuse.

query: How does the protected disclosure mechanism in section 337 of POCA 2002 interact with the reporting obligation in section 330, and what information-sharing restrictions does a qualifying protected disclosure override?
reference_14b: Section 337 of POCA 2002 provides a protected disclosure mechanism that overrides any information-sharing restrictions when certain conditions are met. These conditions include the information coming to the discloser in the course of their trade, profession, business, or employment, causing them to know or suspect, or giving them reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering. Additionally, the disclosure must be made to a constable, customs officer, or nominated officer as soon as practicable. This protected disclosure mechanism aligns with the reporting obligation in section 330, where individuals are required to disclose information to a nominated officer if they know or suspect money laundering activities.
reference_72b: Section 337 of POCA 2002 provides that a disclosure meeting certain conditions is not considered to breach any information-sharing restrictions. These conditions include that the information came to the discloser in the course of their trade, profession, business, or employment, and that it caused the discloser to know or suspect, or gave them reasonable grounds for knowing or suspecting, that another person is engaged in money laundering. The disclosure must be made to a constable, customs officer, or nominated officer as soon as practicable. This protected disclosure mechanism aligns with the reporting obligation in section 330, which requires reporting such suspicions to a nominated officer or authorized person.

query: Section 335 of POCA 2002 defines 'appropriate consent' as consent given in response to an authorised disclosure — what does section 338 require for that underlying disclosure to be authorised?
reference_14b: Section 338 requires that an authorised disclosure must meet one of three conditions: it must be made before the alleged offender does the prohibited act, or if made during the act, it must be done on the offender's initiative as soon as they know or suspect the property is criminal, or if made after the act, it must be due to a reasonable excuse and also made promptly.
reference_72b: For a disclosure to be authorised under section 338, it must be a disclosure to a constable, a customs officer, or a nominated officer by the alleged offender that property is criminal property, and one of three conditions must be met: the disclosure is made before the prohibited act, during the act but as soon as practicable after the alleged offender knows or suspects the property is criminal, or after the act with a reasonable excuse for the delay and as soon as practicable.

query: Under section 336 of POCA 2002, what disclosure must a nominated officer make before giving appropriate consent, and which section of POCA governs that underlying disclosure?
reference_14b: A nominated officer must make a disclosure that property is criminal property to a person authorised for the purposes of this Part by the Director General of the National Crime Agency before giving appropriate consent, and this underlying disclosure is governed by section 338 of POCA 2002.
reference_72b: A nominated officer must make a disclosure that property is criminal property to a person authorised for the purposes of this Part by the Director General of the National Crime Agency before giving appropriate consent. The underlying disclosure is governed by section 338 of POCA 2002.

query: Section 339 of POCA 2002 empowers the Secretary of State to prescribe the form of disclosures — does this prescription apply to disclosures made under section 330, and what is the consequence of non-compliance?
reference_14b: The Secretary of State can prescribe the form and manner in which disclosures under section 330 must be made. If a person makes a disclosure under section 330 in a way that does not conform to the prescribed form or manner, they commit an offense unless they have a reasonable excuse.
reference_72b: The Secretary of State can prescribe the form and manner for disclosures made under section 330, among others. Non-compliance with the prescribed form or manner is an offence unless the person has a reasonable excuse.

query: Section 342 of POCA 2002 creates a tipping off offence where an appropriate officer is conducting an investigation — what types of investigation are defined in section 341 for this purpose?
reference_14b: Section 342 of POCA 2002 creates a tipping off offense when an appropriate officer conducts investigations including confiscation, civil recovery, detained cash, detained property, frozen funds, cryptoasset, exploitation proceeds, or money laundering investigations.
reference_72b: Section 341 defines several types of investigations for the purpose of the tipping off offence under Section 342, including: confiscation investigation, civil recovery investigation, detained cash investigation, detained property investigation, frozen funds investigation, cryptoasset investigation, and money laundering investigation.

query: The section 327 offences in POCA 2002 apply to 'criminal property' — where in POCA is that term defined, and what two elements must be established to show that property qualifies as criminal?
reference_14b: The term 'criminal property' is defined in section 340(3) of POCA 2002. To qualify as criminal property, two elements must be established: first, the property must constitute a person's benefit from criminal conduct or represent such a benefit (in whole or part and whether directly or indirectly); second, the alleged offender must know or suspect that the property constitutes or represents such a benefit.
reference_72b: The term 'criminal property' is defined in section 340 of POCA 2002. To qualify as criminal property, two elements must be established: (1) the property must constitute or represent a person's benefit from criminal conduct, and (2) the alleged offender must know or suspect that the property constitutes or represents such a benefit.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 3 defines 'money laundering' by reference to a specific POCA 2002 provision — which section, and what conduct does that provision describe as money laundering?
reference_14b: Money laundering is defined in MLR 2017 regulation 3 as an act which constitutes an offence under sections 327, 328, or 329 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, or an attempt, conspiracy, or incitement to commit such offences, or aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring the commission of such offences, or conduct that would constitute such offences if done in the United Kingdom.
reference_72b: The MLR 2017 defines 'money laundering' by reference to section 340 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. This section describes money laundering as an act which constitutes an offence under sections 327, 328, or 329 of the POCA 2002, or an attempt, conspiracy, or incitement to commit such an offence, or aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring the commission of such an offence.

query: Regulation 27(1)(c) of MLR 2017 requires a relevant person to apply CDD where it suspects money laundering — how is money laundering defined for the purposes of the Regulations, and where is that definition found?
reference_14b: Money laundering is defined in section 340(11) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. This definition is incorporated by reference in MLR 2017 regulation 3, which defines 'money laundering' as having the meaning given by section 340(11) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002.
reference_72b: Money laundering is defined as the meaning given by section 340(11) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002. This definition is found in MLR 2017 regulation 3, which points to section 340(11) of POCA 2002 for the definition of 'money laundering'.

query: The failure-to-disclose offence in POCA 2002 section 330 applies where a person knows or suspects another is engaged in money laundering — how is 'money laundering' defined in section 340 for this purpose?
reference_14b: Money laundering is defined in section 340(11) as an act that constitutes an offence under sections 327, 328, or 329, an attempt, conspiracy, or incitement to commit such offences, or aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring the commission of such offences. It also includes acts that would constitute these offences if done in the United Kingdom.
reference_72b: Money laundering is defined as an act which constitutes an offence under section 327, 328 or 329, or constitutes an attempt, conspiracy, or incitement to commit such an offence, or constitutes aiding, abetting, counselling, or procuring the commission of such an offence, or would constitute such an offence if done in the United Kingdom.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 33 requires enhanced CDD where risks arise from a case identified in the relevant person's risk assessment under regulation 18 — what must that risk assessment encompass?
reference_14b: The risk assessment must identify and assess the risks of money laundering and terrorist financing to which the business is subject, considering factors relating to customers, countries or geographic areas in which it operates, its products or services, its transactions, and its delivery channels.
reference_72b: The risk assessment under regulation 18 must encompass information made available by the supervisory authority and risk factors related to the relevant person's customers, the countries or geographic areas in which it operates, its products or services, its transactions, and its delivery channels.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 44 requires trustees to maintain records containing information specified in another regulation — which regulation prescribes that information, and what categories of beneficial owner data are required?
reference_14b: MLR 2017 regulation 44 requires trustees to maintain records containing information specified in regulation 45(2)(b) to (d) and (5)(f) and (g). The categories of beneficial owner data required include the individual's full name, national insurance number or unique taxpayer reference, usual residential address, passport or identification card details, date of birth, and the nature of the individual’s role in relation to the trust.
reference_72b: MLR 2017 regulation 44 requires trustees to maintain records containing information specified in regulation 45. The categories of beneficial owner data required include the full name, national insurance number or unique taxpayer reference, residential address, passport or identification card details if the address is not in the UK, date of birth, and the nature of the individual's role in relation to the trust.

query: Section 332 of POCA 2002 creates a failure-to-disclose offence for persons nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338 — what are the conditions of that offence, and how does it differ from the section 330 offence?
reference_14b: Section 332 of POCA 2002 establishes an offense for a person nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338 if they know or suspect another person is engaged in money laundering, the information leading to such knowledge or suspicion came from a disclosure under those sections, they know the identity or whereabouts of laundered property from such disclosures, and they fail to disclose this information to an authorized person as soon as practicable. This differs from the section 330 offence in that section 332 applies specifically to a person nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338 (i.e. someone who has received a report from within their organisation), whereas section 330 applies more broadly to any person in the regulated sector who forms the requisite knowledge or suspicion directly, regardless of whether they hold a nominated-officer role.
reference_72b: Section 332 of POCA 2002 creates a failure-to-disclose offence for persons nominated to receive disclosures under sections 337 or 338. The conditions are: (1) the person knows or suspects another person is engaged in money laundering, (2) the information leading to this knowledge or suspicion came from a disclosure under section 337 or 338, (3) the person knows or can identify the launderer or the whereabouts of the laundered property, or reasonably believes the information will assist in identification, and (4) the person fails to disclose this information to an authorized person as soon as practicable. Unlike the section 330 offence, this applies specifically to nominated officers who receive disclosures.

query: MLR 2017 regulation 3 defines 'nominated officer' as a person nominated to receive disclosures under Part 7 of POCA 2002 — which section in Part 7 imposes the primary obligation to disclose suspicions of money laundering to such a person?
reference_14b: The primary obligation to disclose suspicions of money laundering to a nominated officer is imposed by section 330 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002.
reference_72b: The primary obligation to disclose suspicions of money laundering to a nominated officer is imposed by section 330 of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002.
